text: The ultrasound image displays a swollen appendix measuring nine millimeters in diameter, indicative of appendicitis.
The laparoscopic view shows the cecum with visible tinea coli. The cecum appears quite mobile, and the surgeon follows the tinea coli to locate the appendix.
The surgeon navigates laparoscopically to reach the base of the appendix, visible at the junction with the cecum.
The surgeon lifts the terminal ileum, revealing its position relative to the cecum during the laparoscopic appendectomy.
The surgeon locates the appendix, which is visibly inflamed, during the laparoscopic appendectomy procedure.
The surgeon identifies the base of the appendix, where the three tinea coli converge, noting the appendix's pelvic position.

The appendix is adhered in the pelvis, requiring the surgeon to lift it with the left hand and release the adhesions. The tip of the appendix appears severely inflamed with a contained perforation, surrounded by omentum and fibrinous flecks. Blunt dissection is used to free the appendix tip from the pelvic adhesions.
The surgeon performs blunt dissection, gradually revealing the appendix as the surrounding tissue is separated.
The surgeon uses a grasper in the left hand to hold the appendix and begins dissecting the mesoappendix, separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon applies traction and counter-traction with the left hand to hold the appendix while using the back of a hook to carefully dissect the thick mesoappendix bit by bit.
The surgeon observes the mesoappendix, which appears thick and edematous, indicating inflammation and swelling.

The surgeon maintains traction on the appendix with the left hand while using the back of a hook to carefully dissect the thick and edematous mesoappendix, gradually separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon adjusts the traction applied with the left hand while holding the appendix, facilitating the dissection of the thick and edematous mesoappendix using a hook.
The surgeon continues to dissect the thick and edematous mesoappendix using the back of a hook instrument, applying traction with the left hand to facilitate separation.
The surgeon uses the back of the hook to dissect the thick and edematous mesoappendix, while releasing smoke to maintain clear visibility in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses traction on the appendix, gradually pulling it down to expose the base of the appendix for further dissection.
The surgeon applies traction and counter traction to the mesoappendix, causing it to fall down smoothly and easily, enhancing visibility for further dissection.
The surgeon uses traction with the left hand while placing the back of the hook at the base of the mesoappendix, causing it to fall down.
The surgeon continues to apply traction, allowing the mesoappendix to fall down smoothly until reaching the base of the appendix, where the three tinea coli converge.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the base of the appendix, ensuring not to injure the adjacent cecum during the procedure.
The surgeon uses a handmade loop of 2-0 Vicryl suture, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon creates an extracorporeal knot and uses a Maryland dissector to push the handmade loop into the surgical field.
The surgeon pushes the handmade 2-0 Vicryl loop down onto the appendix, securing it in place.
The surgeon positions the knot at the base of the appendix using the Maryland dissector, ensuring the jaw is angled downward to guide the knot to the desired location.
The surgeon uses the Maryland dissector to push and secure the knot at the confluence of the appendix, ensuring the knot is tightly positioned.
The surgeon secures the knot at the base of the appendix using a Maryland dissector, ensuring it is positioned correctly.
The surgeon secures the knot at the base of the appendix, forming a visible dumbbell shape with the tissue.
The surgeon observes blanching of the appendix, indicating reduced blood flow to the tissue.
The surgeon observes a well-secured knot at the base of the appendix and proceeds to cut the appendix using either a hook or scissors.
The surgeon removes the appendix and prepares to extract it from the left iliac region, using the right hand for support.
The surgeon releases the left hand grasper and uses the right hand to extract the removed appendix through the ten mm port in the left iliac fossa.
The surgeon irrigates the right iliac fossa to clean the area, with minimal fluid present in the surgical field.
The surgeon observes the pelvis, noting its clean appearance following the appendectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses suction to remove fluid from the surgical field, ensuring the stump of the appendix is securely closed.
The surgeon carefully removes the trocar under direct visual guidance, ensuring safe extraction from the surgical site.
The surgeon positions a 10 mm trocar in the left iliac region for support, preparing the area for further surgical actions.
The surgical field is observed to be free of bleeding, indicating hemostasis has been achieved.
The surgeon removes the trocar, followed by the removal of the suprapubic 5mm trocar, ensuring the surgical site is clear.
